summary:
The Seven Years' War (1756–1763 AD) was the first truly global war in history, fought across five continents simultaneously — Europe, North America, South America, West Africa, India, and the Philippines — and resulting in a comprehensive British imperial triumph that established British dominance in North America and India and made Britain the undisputed world maritime and colonial power for the following century. Called by Winston Churchill 'the real first world war,' it involved all the major European powers: Britain and Prussia on one side against France, Austria, Russia, Sweden, and Spain on the other, in a conflict that grew from the overlap of the [[event:2456|French and Indian War]] in North America (where it began in 1754) and the Austrian-Prussian conflict over Silesia in Europe.

In North America, British victories expelled France from Canada (the siege of Quebec, 1759, where both commanding generals Wolfe and Montcalm died) and most of the eastern continent. In India, the [[event:2333|Battle of Plassey]] (1757) gave the East India Company control of Bengal. The [[event:3279|Battle of Rossbach]] (1757) demonstrated Frederick the Great's tactical genius as Prussian forces defeated a much larger Franco-Imperial army. The [[event:2393|Treaty of Paris]] (1763) confirmed British gains: France ceded Canada, most of its North American territories, and Indian interests. The war's long-term consequences were paradoxical: British victory proved costly, as the effort to tax the colonies to pay war debts triggered the [[event:106|American Revolution]] (1776); French defeat proved destabilizing, as war costs contributed to the fiscal crisis that eventually triggered the [[event:107|French Revolution]] (1789). The Seven Years' War thus set in motion the sequence of revolutions that would transform the Atlantic world.
